primary_sources.finding: Repositories created after July 15, 2026 use an immutable default OIDC subject format containing owner and repository IDs; older repositories retain the previous format unless opted in, and renames or transfers after that date move to the immutable format.
The provider discovery document is the authoritative location for supported OIDC claims and signing metadata.

finding: Name-only GitHub Actions OIDC subject allowlists can become stale or vulnerable to namespace reuse. Policies should bind both readable names and immutable numeric owner/repository IDs, while explicitly modeling the migration period for pre-existing repositories.
design_implication: Parse both legacy and immutable subject forms, reject malformed or lookalike values, expose migrationRequired for accepted legacy subjects, and support a fail-closed requireImmutable policy after repository OIDC configuration is migrated.

coverage: legacy compatibility explicitly flagged for migration
legacy subject rejected after immutable enforcement
malformed format and qualifier drift rejected

uncertainty: The repository's live OIDC subject configuration was not queried.
The actual owner_id and repository_id values were not embedded in tests or policy examples.
No live GitHub Actions token was requested or verified in this run.
falsification_route: Request a live GitHub Actions OIDC token for the target workflow.
Verify its signature against the provider JWKS and inspect the exact sub, repository_id, and repository_owner_id claims.
Compare the observed subject with the policy evaluator in legacy-compatible and immutable-required modes.
Reject this finding if current official GitHub documentation or a signed live token contradicts the documented format.
trust_limit: A matching OIDC subject establishes workflow identity attributes only. It does not prove that a deployment, test, peer trigger, or scientific claim succeeded.
